research.product: NetApp ONTAP REST API — whoami identity endpoint (new in ONTAP 9.19.1)
research.interface: GET /api/security/login/whoami with GET /api/cluster version fallback for pre-9.19.1 clusters

official_sources.used_for: the whoami capability ('retrieve the username, role, and permissions information for the logged in user') is new in ONTAP 9.19.1 — older clusters do not serve it

official_sources.title: Retrieve the cluster configuration (GET /api/cluster)

official_sources.title: Getting started with the ONTAP REST API (NetApp official docs source)
official_sources.used_for: base path /api, HTTP basic authentication, fields query parameter, and the {error:{code,message,target}} error object used to distinguish 401/403/404 responses

research.language_rationale: Java is where our fleet tooling lives, and ONTAP's documented integration surface for it is the raw REST API (NetApp's own clients are generated from the same OpenAPI contract). java.net.http.HttpClient exercises the documented surface hermetically with zero dependencies, and Java's default TLS validation is exactly the behavior this probe must never disable.

verified_facts: ONTAP 9.19.1 adds a whoami capability that returns the username, role, and permissions of the logged-in user; clusters older than 9.19.1 do not serve the endpoint.
The endpoint is GET /api/security/login/whoami; its response carries username, roles (array of role names) and privileges (array of {path, access} entries such as {"path": "/api", "access": "all"}).
ONTAP GET resources accept an explicit fields query parameter to select returned properties.

GET /api/cluster returns the cluster name and a version object with full, generation, major and minor — available on all supported ONTAP releases and the correct capability fallback for pre-9.19.1 clusters.

Errors use the documented {error:{code,message,target}} object; HTTP 403 means the authenticated user lacks permission (an error to surface), while HTTP 404 on the whoami path means the endpoint does not exist on that cluster (fall back, not an error); HTTP 401 is an authentication failure and must not fall back.
All requests use HTTP basic authentication against the /api base path over TLS; certificate validation must never be disabled.
